visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A grey target shape made of eight squares (a three-by-three block missing one corner), beside four options that each show two separate multi-square pieces.

items.construct_rationale: Form assembly — combining parts into a whole — is a classic spatial-visualization task (the form-board tradition). Requiring exact tiling with mental rotation, and defeating the area-count shortcut with footprint-impossible foils, forces true spatial fit reasoning rather than arithmetic.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond counting small squares and the idea that a piece can be turned.

ambiguity_review.notes: Target = 3x3 minus the top-right cell (8 cells). Verified A tiles it: 2x2 block in the upper-left quadrant covers (0,0),(1,0),(0,1),(1,1); the L-tetromino rotated covers (0,2),(1,2),(2,2),(2,1) — together exactly the eight target cells, no overlap, no gap, rotation only (no flip needed; the L's handedness matches). Verified each foil is impossible for a certain reason: B contains a piece with a straight arm of four cells and C contains a straight four-in-a-line piece — the target's bounding box is only 3x3, so no four-collinear-cell segment can be placed, making both impossible despite matching the 8-cell area; D totals 9 cells and cannot fit an 8-cell target. No second option can tile the target, so the answer is unique. Reflections are explicitly disallowed in the prompt, removing any handedness ambiguity.

source_basis: Form assembly / part-whole construction as a spatial-visualization measure (Thurstone 1938; Wechsler Object Assembly tradition)
Polyomino tiling and bounding-footprint constraints; area-matched non-fitting distractors to defeat counting heuristics (Carroll 1993 spatial factor)

items.technical_construct: Mental rotation (3D rigid rotation of a multi-cube object)
items.subskill: Identifying a true 3D rotation of a chiral cube figure and rejecting its mirror image (enantiomorph) and structurally different foils
items.difficulty_rationale: A Shepard-Metzler-style object: eight unit cubes joined in a chain with two right-angle bends lying in two different planes, which makes the object chiral (its mirror image cannot be rotated onto it). The solver must build the 3D structure mentally, rotate it, and decide which option is the same object. The decisive foil is the enantiomorph (option B) — the single hardest distractor in the spatial literature, because reversed handedness defeats every feature-matching or partial-rotation strategy. This is genuine ceiling spatial load, not clutter: the object is only eight cubes with two bends, so the difficulty comes from the 3D rotation-plus-handedness reasoning rather than from visual density. The exact coordinates below make the item uniquely solvable and deterministically renderable; the ONLY residual risk is mobile legibility (eight cubes across five panels). The 5 rating is therefore contingent on the rendered asset passing the post-render legibility check described in mobile_behavior and ambiguity_review. If, after rendering at the specified edge length, individual cubes are not separable on a ~360px-wide phone screen even with zoom, downgrade to Difficulty 4 — the logic stays valid, but perceptual difficulty would otherwise inflate the construct.
items.prompt: The object on the left is shown again — turned to a new position — in exactly one of the four options. The others are either a mirror image or a different object. Which option is the SAME object, only rotated?
items.solution: The target is a chain of eight cubes whose step-to-step moves are [+x,+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z]: a run of three cubes along +x, a turn upward for a run of three (two +y steps), then a turn into depth for a run of four (three +z steps). The two turns lie in different planes, giving the object a fixed handedness (right-handed screw sense). OPTION A is this same object after a proper rotation R_y(+90 degrees), matrix [[0,0,1],[0,1,0],[-1,0,0]] i.e. (x,y,z)->(z,y,-x), then a normalizing shift; its moves become [-z,-z,+y,+y,+x,+x,+x] — identical structure and identical handedness, so A is a true rotation and is the answer. OPTION B is the target reflected through the z=0 plane, (x,y,z)->(x,y,-z); its moves are [+x,+x,+y,+y,-z,-z,-z] — same run lengths and bend angles but reversed handedness, so it is the mirror image and cannot be produced by any rotation. OPTION C has three bends, not two (moves [+x,+x,+y,+z,+z,-y,-y]), so it is a structurally different object. OPTION D has run lengths of 2, 3 and 5 cubes (moves [+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z,+z], a run-step multiset of {1,2,4} versus the target's {2,2,3}), so it is also a different object. Only A preserves both the connectivity and the handedness of the target.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: a proper rotation (R_y(+90 degrees)) of the target — same connectivity, same run lengths, same handedness.
distractor_rationales.B: The enantiomorph (mirror image through the z=0 plane). Identical run lengths and bend angles but opposite handedness; no rotation can reproduce it. The hardest foil — traps anyone who matches arm lengths without checking screw sense.
distractor_rationales.C: Structurally different: it has three right-angle bends instead of two, so it is not congruent to the target under any rigid motion.
distractor_rationales.D: Structurally different: its straight runs are 2, 3 and 5 cubes long (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) rather than the target's 3, 3 and 4 cubes ({2,2,3}), so it is a different object.

visual_asset_spec.coordinate_system: Right-handed integer cube lattice. Each tuple is the integer position of one unit cube; x = right, y = up, z = depth (away from viewer). Cubes are listed in chain order (cube 1 to cube 8); consecutive cubes differ by exactly 1 in one axis. All coordinate lists are normalized so the minimum value on each axis is 0.

transform_provenance.A_from_target: A = R_y(+90 degrees) applied to each target cube, then translate by (0,0,+2) to normalize. R_y(+90) = [[0,0,1],[0,1,0],[-1,0,0]]; equivalently (x,y,z)->(z,y,-x). det = +1 (proper rotation), so A is the SAME object reoriented.
transform_provenance.B_from_target: B = reflection M applied to each target cube, then translate by (0,0,+3). M = diag(1,1,-1); equivalently (x,y,z)->(x,y,-z), a reflection in the z=0 plane. det = -1 (improper). Because the object is chiral, B is the enantiomorph and is NOT reachable by any rotation.
transform_provenance.C_structure: C is authored directly (not a transform of the target). Move sequence [+x,+x,+y,+z,+z,-y,-y] gives THREE right-angle bends, versus the target's two. Bend count is a rigid-motion invariant, so C is a different object.
transform_provenance.D_structure: D is authored directly. Move sequence [+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z,+z] gives straight runs of 2, 3 and 5 cubes (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) versus the target's runs of 3, 3 and 4 cubes ({2,2,3}). Run-length multiset is a rigid-motion invariant, so D is a different object.

visual_asset_spec.projection: Render every cube with a single fixed isometric projection — do NOT apply any additional render-time rotation; each option's orientation is fully encoded in its coordinates. For a lattice point (x,y,z): screenX = ORIGIN_X + (x - z) * 0.8660 * E; screenY = ORIGIN_Y + (x + z) * 0.5 * E - y * E, where E = cube edge in px (use E = 28). Draw each unit cube as the standard isometric hexagon: a top rhombus plus a left and a right side-face. Painter's order: the view direction is from (+1,+1,+1) toward the origin, so sort cubes by ascending (x + y + z) and draw in that order, letting nearer cubes (larger x + y + z) paint last (on top).
visual_asset_spec.shading_and_stroke: Tokens from design-tokens.json. Cube edges: ink #14181F, stroke 2 (round join). Face fills, three flat tones for solidity: top faces #FFFFFF (paper), left faces #F5F6F8 (paper_tint), right faces #E7E9EE (a light grey between paper_tint and fill_muted). No accent colour needed. Outline the full silhouette of each object slightly heavier (stroke 2.5) so the chain reads as one solid against the white ground.
visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: STIMULUS: the 'target' object drawn alone at the top (or left) using object_coordinates.target and the projection above. Then a divider, then the four option tiles. OPTION TILES: each a fixed-size panel (recommend 180x200) rendering, respectively, object_coordinates.A, .B, .C, .D, each centred in its panel using the SAME E, the SAME projection, and the SAME painter's rule. Label tiles A, B, C, D. Every cube must show all of its visible edges; where one cube partially hides another, keep the front cube's edges crisp so the count of eight cubes stays recoverable.

option_descriptions.A: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.A.
option_descriptions.B: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.B.
option_descriptions.C: An eight-cube chain with three right-angle bends, drawn from object_coordinates.C.
option_descriptions.D: An eight-cube chain with two right-angle bends and different run lengths, drawn from object_coordinates.D.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Stimulus stacks above the option grid below ~420px; options render 2x2. This is the most demanding item to read on a phone, so zoom is mandatory and enabled by default on this item. Occlusion mitigation: (1) the fixed (+1,+1,+1) camera plus the chosen coordinates keep every chain spread across the projection rather than stacked toward the viewer, so no cube is fully hidden; (2) full per-cube edge outlining and the three-tone face shading keep adjacent cubes separable; (3) options are never re-rotated at render time, so the apparent orientation is exactly the coordinates. POST-RENDER CHECK (required before acceptance): rasterize each tile at 360px panel width and confirm all eight cubes are countable and the silhouette of A is distinguishable from B; if not, increase E or the panel size, and if it still fails, downgrade the item to Difficulty 4.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A three-dimensional object built from eight small cubes joined in a bent chain, shown beside four other cube-chain objects drawn at different orientations.

items.construct_rationale: Rotating a three-dimensional asymmetric object in the mind and distinguishing it from its enantiomorph is the canonical ceiling measure of spatial ability (Shepard & Metzler; Vandenberg & Kuse Mental Rotations Test). It simultaneously loads 3D structure-building, rotation, and handedness checking. Exact lattice coordinates and a single fixed projection remove the viewing-angle ambiguity that weakened version 1.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond perceiving a line drawing as a 3D object and understanding the difference between 'turned' and 'mirror image'.

ambiguity_review.notes: Proofs use rigid-motion invariants on the move sequences (target moves M_T = [+x,+x,+y,+y,+z,+z,+z]). (A is a true rotation) A = R_y(+90)·target with det = +1; a proper rotation maps an object to a congruent copy of the SAME handedness, so A is the target reoriented. (B is a mirror, not a rotation) B = diag(1,1,-1)·target with det = -1. Suppose a rotation R mapped target onto B reading the chain in the same order: R would have to satisfy R(+x)=+x, R(+y)=+y, R(+z)=-z, i.e. R = diag(1,1,-1), which has det = -1 and is not a rotation — contradiction. Reading B's chain in reverse order gives moves [+z,+z,+z,-y,-y,-x,-x]; matching target's forward moves would force R(+x) to equal both +z and -y, impossible. So no rotation (under either chain orientation) maps target to B; B is the enantiomorph. (C is structurally different) C has three right-angle bends versus the target's two; bend count is invariant under all rigid motions, so C is not congruent to the target. (D is structurally different) D's straight-run lengths are 2, 3, 5 cubes (run-step multiset {1,2,4}) versus the target's 3, 3, 4 cubes ({2,2,3}); the run-length multiset is invariant under rigid motions, so D is not congruent to the target. (Exactly one valid) Of the four, only A is congruent-by-rotation to the target: B is a reflection, C and D are not congruent at all. Therefore exactly one option is correct. Residual risk is perceptual only (mobile occlusion), addressed by the required post-render legibility check in mobile_behavior; it does not affect single-answer uniqueness.

source_basis: 3D mental rotation and enantiomorph (mirror) foils (Shepard & Metzler 1971)
Mirror-image versus rotated-image discrimination as the ceiling spatial item type (Vandenberg & Kuse 1978, Mental Rotations Test; Carroll 1993 spatial taxonomy)

items.subskill: Identifying a recursive generating rule and extending it one term
items.difficulty_rationale: A short series with one consistent recursive rule (each term is double the previous plus one) that is independently confirmed by the first-difference pattern (3, 6, 12, 24 — each difference doubles). Two natural, low-complexity rule families converge on the same next term, so the answer is well-determined while the reasoning is a single step of rule induction. Light arithmetic, no large numbers. Easy end of the range.
items.prompt:
What number continues the sequence?

2, 5, 11, 23, 47, ?
items.solution: Each term is double the previous term plus one: 2x2+1=5, 5x2+1=11, 11x2+1=23, 23x2+1=47, so the next term is 47x2+1 = 95. The same answer falls out of the differences (3, 6, 12, 24), which double each step: the next difference is 48, and 47+48 = 95. Both readings agree, so the answer is 95.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: 47x2+1 = 95 (also 47 + 48 = 95 via the doubling differences).
distractor_rationales.B: 94: applied 'double the previous term' but dropped the +1 (47x2).
distractor_rationales.C: 71: continued the differences with the last difference unchanged (47 + 24) instead of doubling it to 48.
distractor_rationales.D: 96: doubled and added 2 instead of 1 (47x2+2), a slip in the additive constant.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the sequence inline in the prompt using the UI numeric font; place the five given terms and the '?' on one line separated by commas with comfortable spacing. Optionally render the '?' in the muted grey token (#9AA2B1) to match the question-mark styling used elsewhere.

option_descriptions.A: The number 95.
option_descriptions.B: The number 94.
option_descriptions.C: The number 71.
option_descriptions.D: The number 96.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Pure text; the sequence fits one line at mobile width. Options render as four tappable number chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A sequence of five numbers followed by a question mark, with four numeric answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Number-series completion is a standard marker of inductive reasoning applied to quantity (the ICAR / Thurstone number-series tradition). Using a rule that is corroborated by two independent readings keeps the answer objective.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond doubling and adding small whole numbers.

ambiguity_review.notes: Uniqueness argument (over plausible low-complexity rules): the rule a(n+1)=2a(n)+1 fits all four observed transitions exactly, and the equivalent first-difference rule (differences 3,6,12,24, each x2) also fits and predicts the same next difference (48). Both give 95. Common competing simple rules are ruled out by the data: a constant difference fails (differences are not constant), a constant ratio fails (5/2, 11/5, ... are not equal), and add-a-fixed-number fails. While any finite series can be fit by a high-degree polynomial, no rule of complexity at or below the intended one yields a value other than 95; each distractor corresponds to a specific identifiable mis-step, not a rival global rule.

source_basis: Number-series items as inductive-reasoning measures (Thurstone 1938 Number factor; ICAR number series, Condon & Revelle 2014)
Recursive (multiply-and-add) generating rules with corroborating difference structure (standard quantitative-reasoning item design)

items.subskill: Inferring a two-input arithmetic rule from worked rows and applying it
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must infer a rule that combines the two left-hand numbers in each row to produce the right-hand number, using two fully worked rows as evidence, then apply it to the third. The rule (sum of the two squares) is not the first thing most people try, and the two worked rows over-determine it — they eliminate the tempting 'product' and 'square of the sum' readings. Discovering a non-obvious two-input rule from examples is solid medium difficulty; the arithmetic itself stays small.
items.prompt:
In each row, the third number is produced from the first two by the same rule. What number replaces the question mark?

Row 1:  3,  4  ->  25
Row 2:  5,  2  ->  29
Row 3:  6,  3  ->  ?
items.solution: The third number is the sum of the squares of the first two. Row 1: 3^2 + 4^2 = 9 + 16 = 25. Row 2: 5^2 + 2^2 = 25 + 4 = 29. Both check out, so Row 3 is 6^2 + 3^2 = 36 + 9 = 45.

distractor_rationales.B: 81: squared the sum, (6+3)^2 = 81 — a common mis-reading of 'squares', and it fails on the given rows ((3+4)^2 = 49, not 25).
distractor_rationales.C: 39: squared only the first number and added the second, 6^2 + 3 = 39.
distractor_rationales.D: 18: multiplied the two numbers, 6 x 3 = 18, ignoring the squaring (and inconsistent with the worked rows).

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No drawing needed. Present the three rows as a clean three-column table (first number, second number, result) or as the aligned text shown in the prompt. Use the UI numeric font; right-align numbers; render the '?' in muted grey (#9AA2B1). Keep generous row spacing so the two example rows read clearly as worked examples and the third as the unknown.

option_descriptions.A: The number 45.
option_descriptions.B: The number 81.
option_descriptions.C: The number 39.
option_descriptions.D: The number 18.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: The table is three short rows of small numbers and fits mobile width without scrolling. Options render as four number chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A small table with three rows; each of the first two rows shows two numbers and their result, and the third row shows two numbers and a question mark.

items.construct_rationale: Inferring a function of two inputs from worked examples is a clean inductive-reasoning task over quantity, distinct from a single-stream series because the solver must consider how two operands combine. Two worked rows make the rule identifiable rather than guessable.
items.knowledge_requirements: Squaring small whole numbers and adding. No algebra notation required.

ambiguity_review.notes: Two worked rows constrain the rule. Sum-of-squares fits both (25 and 29). Checked the natural competitors against BOTH rows: product (a*b) gives 12 and 10 (fail); square of the sum ((a+b)^2) gives 49 and 49 (fail); a^2 + b gives 13 and 27 (fail); a + b^2 gives 19 and 9 (fail); a*b + (a+b) gives 19 and 17 (fail). Only a^2 + b^2 satisfies both example rows, so the rule is identified and Row 3 = 45 is forced. Each distractor is the output of one of the eliminated rules or a partial application, i.e. a real reasoning slip rather than a rival rule that also fits the data.

source_basis: Two-operand rule induction from examples (inductive-reasoning item tradition; Carroll 1993, Induction factor)
Sum-of-squares and square-of-sum as classic confusable arithmetic rules used in pattern items

items.technical_construct: Quantitative reasoning (proportional reasoning with combined direct and inverse relations)
items.subskill: Combining a direct proportion (output) with an inverse proportion (workers) and tracking direction
items.difficulty_rationale: The solver must integrate two proportional relationships that pull in opposite directions: more fences means more time (direct), but more painters means less time (inverse). The difficulty is structural — recognising which quantity scales which way and combining them correctly — rather than computational; the numbers are deliberately small and divide cleanly. The distractors capture the three classic errors (drop one of the two scalings, or invert the worker relationship), which is what makes it a genuine Difficulty 4 reasoning item rather than an arithmetic exercise.
items.prompt: 3 painters can paint 2 fences in 4 hours. Working at the same steady rate, how many hours will 6 painters take to paint 8 fences?
items.solution: Find the rate per painter. 3 painters paint 2 fences in 4 hours, so the work rate is 2 fences / (3 painters x 4 hours) = 1/6 of a fence per painter-hour. With 6 painters the team paints 6 x 1/6 = 1 fence per hour. To paint 8 fences therefore takes 8 / 1 = 8 hours.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: rate is 1/6 fence per painter-hour; 6 painters do 1 fence/hour; 8 fences take 8 hours.
distractor_rationales.B: 16: scaled the time up for the extra fences (8/2 = 4x, giving 16 hours) but ignored that the painters also doubled.
distractor_rationales.C: 2: accounted for doubling the painters but forgot the fences increased — computed the time for 6 painters to paint the original 2 fences.
distractor_rationales.D: 32: scaled by both ratios but treated more painters as MORE time (4 x 4 x 2) instead of less, inverting the worker relationship.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the problem as a single short paragraph in the UI font. No table or diagram is needed; keep numerals as digits for quick scanning.

option_descriptions.A: 8 hours.
option_descriptions.B: 16 hours.
option_descriptions.C: 2 hours.
option_descriptions.D: 32 hours.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Two short sentences; wraps cleanly on mobile. Options render as four chips (with the word 'hours' or a unit label), 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A word problem about painters, fences, and hours, with four numeric answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Proportional reasoning that combines a direct and an inverse relationship is a core quantitative-reasoning skill (Gq) and a classic developmental marker. Keeping the arithmetic trivial isolates the reasoning: the solver is tested on structuring the relationships, not on calculation.
items.knowledge_requirements: Basic multiplication and division of small numbers and the everyday notions of rate and work. No formula memorisation.

ambiguity_review.notes: The constant-rate (man-hours) model is the standard and intended interpretation and yields a single value: time = (fences) x (painter-hours per fence) / (painters) = 8 x 6 / 6 = 8 hours. The phrase 'same steady rate' fixes the linear model and rules out start-up or diminishing-return readings. Each distractor is the result of a specific, nameable deviation (drop the painter scaling -> 16; drop the fence scaling -> 2; invert the painter scaling -> 32), so no distractor arises from an equally valid alternative model. Numbers chosen so the correct answer and all error answers are clean integers, avoiding any rounding ambiguity.

source_basis: Proportional and inverse-proportional reasoning as a quantitative-reasoning factor (Carroll 1993, Gq; Piagetian proportional-reasoning tradition)
Rate / work problems with combined direct and inverse scaling (standard quantitative aptitude item design)

items.subskill: Translating verbal numeric constraints into relations and using an ordering constraint to force a unique solution
items.difficulty_rationale: A strong Difficulty 4. The solver must translate four interlocking verbal statements into relations, combine the three equalities into a single reduced relation (h3 + 2h4 = 10), enumerate the small candidate set it allows, and then apply the remaining ordering constraint ('the third is the smallest') to eliminate all but one candidate. The integration of multiple constraints plus a side ordering condition is what lifts it clearly above a routine multi-step item. It is NOT Difficulty 5, however, because the equalities collapse cleanly to one linear relation and the search space is only four cases that can be scanned by hand; there is no irreducible system or large search to manage. Arithmetic is trivial throughout.
items.prompt:
Four houses stand in a row. Each has a different house number, and every number is a whole number from 1 to 9.

- The four numbers add up to 20.
- The first house's number equals the sum of the third and fourth.
- The second house's number is double the fourth's.
- The third house has the smallest number of the four.

What is the third house's number?
items.solution: Let the numbers be h1, h2, h3, h4. From the clues: h1 = h3 + h4 and h2 = 2 x h4. The total gives h1 + h2 + h3 + h4 = 20, so (h3 + h4) + 2h4 + h3 + h4 = 20, which simplifies to 2h3 + 4h4 = 20, i.e. h3 + 2h4 = 10. Test the few possibilities (h4 must be 1-4 to keep h3 positive): h4=4 -> h3=2, h2=8, h1=6, giving {6,8,2,4}; h4=3 -> h3=4 with set {7,6,4,3}; h4=2 -> h3=6 with set {8,4,6,2}; h4=1 -> h3=8 with set {9,2,8,1}. The last clue requires h3 to be the smallest of the four. Only the first case qualifies (2 is the smallest of 6,8,2,4); in every other case h3 is not the smallest. So h3 = 2.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: the only assignment where the third number is also the smallest gives h3 = 2.
distractor_rationales.B: 4: comes from the h4=3 branch (numbers 7,6,4,3) — reached by solving the equalities but skipping the 'third is the smallest' check, or by reading off the fourth house in the correct solution.
distractor_rationales.C: 6: the first house's number in the correct solution (answered the wrong house), and also the h3 value of the h4=2 branch.
distractor_rationales.D: 8: the second house's number in the correct solution (answered the wrong house), and also the h3 value of the h4=1 branch.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure required. Render the setup sentence followed by the four constraints as a short bulleted list in the UI font, then the question. Keep each constraint on its own line for scannability. A row of four small empty boxes labelled 1st-4th could optionally be shown above the clues for orientation, but it is not necessary and should be omitted if it adds clutter on mobile.

option_descriptions.A: The number 2.
option_descriptions.B: The number 4.
option_descriptions.C: The number 6.
option_descriptions.D: The number 8.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Four short bullet lines plus a one-line question; fits mobile width with no scrolling beyond the clue list. Options render as four number chips, 2 columns on mobile. No zoom needed.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A logic puzzle describing four house numbers with four numeric constraints, followed by four answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Constraint-satisfaction over numbers measures deductive quantitative reasoning: representing relations, combining them, and using a side constraint to select among candidates. It complements the inductive items (sequence, pattern) by testing deduction rather than rule discovery, while keeping computation minimal.
items.knowledge_requirements: Addition, doubling, and comparing small whole numbers. No algebraic technique is required, though it can be solved with light algebra.

ambiguity_review.notes: Uniqueness verified by exhaustion. The equalities reduce the system to h3 + 2h4 = 10 with h1 = h3 + h4 and h2 = 2h4, all distinct whole numbers in 1-9. h4 must be 1,2,3, or 4 (h4>=5 forces h3<=0). The four candidates are: h4=4 -> {h1,h2,h3,h4} = {6,8,2,4}; h4=3 -> {7,6,4,3}; h4=2 -> {8,4,6,2}; h4=1 -> {9,2,8,1}. All four satisfy the sum, the two equalities, the 1-9 range, and distinctness. The ordering constraint 'h3 is the smallest' is satisfied ONLY by the h4=4 case (h3=2 is the minimum of {6,8,2,4}); in the other three, h3 is not the minimum. Hence the full set of constraints has exactly one solution and h3 = 2 is forced. Each distractor is a value present in the solution under a wrong question reading (h1=6, h2=8) or in a rejected branch (h3=4 when the ordering clue is ignored), so distractors reflect genuine slips, not alternative valid solutions.

source_basis: Deductive constraint-satisfaction reasoning with numeric variables (analytic/deductive reasoning tradition; Carroll 1993, Sequential Reasoning)
Use of an inequality/ordering constraint to force uniqueness in small integer systems (standard logic-puzzle item design)

items.subskill: Drawing a valid categorical conclusion and rejecting illicit conversions
items.difficulty_rationale: A two-premise categorical syllogism in the valid Celarent form (All A are B; No B are C; therefore No A are C). One chaining step yields the conclusion. The distractors are the standard categorical errors — illicit conversion of a premise and an unwarranted existential claim — so the item tests whether the solver follows the direction of the relations rather than just word-matching. Single inference, neutral made-up rule, easy end of the range.
items.prompt:
Assume both statements are true:

1. Every member of the Zorn Club wears a blue hat.
2. No one wearing a blue hat is allowed into the library.

Which statement MUST be true?
items.option_a: No member of the Zorn Club is allowed into the library.
items.option_b: Some members of the Zorn Club are allowed into the library.
items.option_c: Anyone who is not allowed into the library is a member of the Zorn Club.
items.option_d: Everyone who wears a blue hat is a member of the Zorn Club.
items.solution: Every Zorn Club member wears a blue hat (premise 1), and no blue-hat wearer is allowed into the library (premise 2). So every Zorn Club member is barred from the library: no member is allowed in. That is option A.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: chaining 'all members wear blue hats' with 'no blue-hat wearer is allowed in' gives 'no member is allowed in.'
distractor_rationales.B: Directly contradicts the valid conclusion; chosen by treating the categories as overlapping when the premises forbid it.
distractor_rationales.C: Reverses premise 2 (illicit conversion): being barred from the library does not make someone a Zorn Club member — many non-members could also be barred.
distractor_rationales.D: Reverses premise 1 (illicit conversion): all members wear blue hats, but that does not mean every blue-hat wearer is a member.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the two numbered premises on separate lines, then the question. Use the UI font; keep the premises visually grouped so they read as a single 'given' block.

option_descriptions.A: Statement: no Zorn Club member is allowed into the library.
option_descriptions.B: Statement: some Zorn Club members are allowed into the library.
option_descriptions.C: Statement: anyone barred from the library is a Zorn Club member.
option_descriptions.D: Statement: every blue-hat wearer is a Zorn Club member.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Short text; premises and options wrap cleanly. Options render as four full-width rows on mobile (text answers, single column) for readability.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: Two logical premises about club members, blue hats, and a library, followed by four statements to evaluate.

items.construct_rationale: Categorical syllogisms are a foundational measure of deductive reasoning. Using abstract, fully stipulated categories prevents reliance on world knowledge, so only the logical form determines the answer.
items.knowledge_requirements: None. The terms are arbitrary labels; no knowledge of clubs, hats, or libraries is needed beyond what the premises state.

ambiguity_review.notes: The premises have the form All Z are H; No H are L. Valid conclusion: No Z are L (option A) — the only statement entailed. Checked each distractor: B contradicts A; C is the converse of premise 2 (not entailed, since L's complement is not pinned to Z); D is the converse of premise 1 (not entailed). None of B, C, D follows from the premises, so exactly one option must be true. No real-world assumption is required because both premises are stipulated as true.

source_basis: Categorical syllogistic reasoning and validity (Aristotelian logic; Johnson-Laird mental-model account of syllogisms)
Illicit conversion as a canonical syllogistic error (standard deductive-reasoning item design)

items.subskill: Interpreting a necessary condition signalled by 'unless', chaining a conditional, and distinguishing necessary from sufficient
items.difficulty_rationale: The first statement uses 'unless', which makes clearance a NECESSARY condition for entering (entering implies clearance), not a sufficient one — the classic point of confusion. The solver must read that direction correctly, chain it with a second conditional (clearance implies fingerprinted), and apply it to a stated fact. The distractors are the three standard conditional errors: treating a necessary condition as sufficient, affirming the consequent (converse), and contradicting the derived fact. Medium difficulty driven by the 'unless'/necessary-condition direction plus a one-step chain.
items.prompt:
Assume both statements are true:

1. No one enters the vault unless they have security clearance.
2. Everyone who has security clearance has been fingerprinted.

Maria entered the vault. Which statement MUST be true?
items.option_b: Having security clearance is enough to let someone enter the vault.
items.option_c: Everyone who has been fingerprinted may enter the vault.
items.option_d: Maria does not have security clearance.
items.solution: 'No one enters the vault unless they have clearance' means entering REQUIRES clearance: since Maria entered, she must have clearance. Statement 2 says everyone with clearance has been fingerprinted. So Maria, having clearance, has been fingerprinted. That is option A.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: entered -> has clearance (from 'unless', a necessary condition) -> has been fingerprinted (statement 2).
distractor_rationales.B: Treats the necessary condition as sufficient. 'Unless' makes clearance necessary for entry, not a guarantee of entry; the statements never say clearance is enough.
distractor_rationales.C: Affirms the consequent / reverses statement 2: being fingerprinted does not imply clearance, and certainly not permission to enter.
distractor_rationales.D: Contradicts the valid chain: since Maria entered, she must HAVE clearance, not lack it.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the two numbered conditionals, then the fact ('Maria entered the vault.'), then the question, each visually separated. Keep 'unless' in the first statement in normal weight (do not bold or otherwise cue it).

option_descriptions.A: Statement: Maria has been fingerprinted.
option_descriptions.B: Statement: clearance is sufficient for entry.
option_descriptions.C: Statement: every fingerprinted person may enter.
option_descriptions.D: Statement: Maria lacks clearance.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Short text block; wraps cleanly. Options render single-column full-width on mobile because they are sentences.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: Two conditional statements about entering a vault, clearance, and fingerprinting, a fact about Maria, and four statements to evaluate.

items.construct_rationale: Conditional reasoning, especially the necessary-versus-sufficient distinction signalled by words like 'unless', is a core deductive skill and a well-documented source of systematic error (Wason selection-task tradition). Chaining two conditionals and applying them to a fact isolates valid inference from the tempting converse.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond ordinary reading of 'unless', 'must', and 'enough'. No security-domain knowledge is used; the rules are fully stipulated.

ambiguity_review.notes: Formalization: enter -> clearance (from 'No one enters unless they have clearance'); clearance -> fingerprinted. Given enter(Maria), modus ponens twice yields fingerprinted(Maria) = option A, the only entailed statement. B fails because neither premise asserts clearance -> enter (necessary is not sufficient). C fails because it reverses statement 2 (fingerprinted -> clearance is not given). D fails because it contradicts the derived clearance(Maria). Exactly one option is forced. Wording was revised from 'You may enter the vault only if you have security clearance' to 'No one enters the vault unless they have security clearance' so the stated fact (Maria actually entered) connects directly to the rule: actual entry, not merely permission to enter, now entails clearance. The necessary-condition reading is preserved ('No X unless Y' = X -> Y).

source_basis: Conditional reasoning and necessary/sufficient conditions (Wason selection task; standard logic of 'unless' as a necessary condition)
Affirming-the-consequent and necessary-as-sufficient as canonical conditional errors (deductive-reasoning item design)

items.subskill: Deriving a forced fact from ordering constraints when the full order is not unique
items.difficulty_rationale: A five-position ordering with four constraints whose full order is not unique — two arrangements satisfy them — so a 'cannot be determined' distractor is available to tempt solvers who conflate an underdetermined whole order with an undetermined first place. However, first place is forced by a short, direct elimination that does not require enumerating the feasible orders: T is last; S is not first; R follows Q so is not first; Q follows P so is not first; therefore P is first. Because the forced fact is reachable in a few one-step exclusions, the item is a solid Difficulty 3 rather than a 4 — the 'cannot be determined' trap is real but defeatable without reasoning over the full set of models. Light, structural, no arithmetic.
items.prompt:
Five runners — P, Q, R, S, and T — finished a race in positions 1st through 5th, with no ties. From the results:

- Q finished immediately ahead of R (Q's position is exactly one before R's).
- P finished somewhere ahead of Q.
- T finished last.
- S did not finish first.

Who MUST have finished first?
items.option_d: It cannot be determined from the information given.
items.solution: T is 5th. Q must sit immediately before R, so the (Q,R) block occupies positions (1,2), (2,3), or (3,4). It cannot be (1,2), because P must be ahead of Q and nothing is ahead of position 1. If (Q,R) = (2,3), then P must be 1st and S takes 4th: order P, Q, R, S, T. If (Q,R) = (3,4), then P must be ahead of Q (1st or 2nd) and S is not 1st, forcing S into 2nd and P into 1st: order P, S, Q, R, T. Both feasible orders put P first. So P must have finished first.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: in every arrangement consistent with the clues, P is 1st.
distractor_rationales.B: Q: chosen by starting the 'P ahead of Q, Q before R' chain at Q and forgetting that P must precede Q, so someone is always ahead of Q.
distractor_rationales.C: S: chosen by misreading 'S did not finish first' or by assuming S could take an early slot; in fact S is never first.
distractor_rationales.D: Cannot be determined: the trap. The full finishing order is indeed not unique (two orders fit), but first place is the same in both, so it IS determined.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure required. Render the setup sentence, then the four constraints as a bulleted list (one per line), then the question. A row of five small position labels (1st-5th) may optionally be shown for orientation but is not necessary; omit if it adds clutter on mobile.

option_descriptions.A: Answer: P.
option_descriptions.B: Answer: Q.
option_descriptions.C: Answer: S.
option_descriptions.D: Answer: cannot be determined.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Four short bullet lines plus a one-line question fit mobile width. Options render as four rows; A/B/C are single letters and D is a short phrase, so use single-column full-width rows for alignment.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: An ordering puzzle about five runners finishing a race, with four answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Linear-ordering deduction (the 'logic games' family) measures the ability to combine relational constraints and reason about what is forced versus what merely varies. Asking for a forced fact under a non-unique order targets the harder, more diagnostic version of the skill.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond understanding race positions and 'immediately ahead of', 'ahead of', 'last', and 'first'.

ambiguity_review.notes: Exhaustive check. T=5 is fixed. The (Q,R) adjacency block can be at (1,2), (2,3), or (3,4). (1,2) is impossible because P must precede Q. (2,3) forces P=1, S=4 (S not first is satisfied): order P,Q,R,S,T. (3,4) forces P and S into positions 1,2 with S not first, hence S=2, P=1: order P,S,Q,R,T. These are the only two feasible orders, and P is 1st in both, so 'P finished first' is forced and unique; options B and C are first in no feasible order, and D is wrong because first place is determined even though the complete order is not. Independent of enumeration, P is also forced first by direct elimination: T is last, S is not first, R follows Q, and Q follows P, leaving only P for first. Exactly one option must be true.

source_basis: Linear ordering / sequencing deduction (analytical-reasoning 'logic games' tradition)
Forced-inference (must-be-true) reasoning over the set of feasible models (deductive-reasoning factor; Carroll 1993, Sequential Reasoning)

items.technical_construct: Deductive reasoning (constraint-satisfaction matching with an embedded conditional)
items.subskill: Resolving a matching grid where a conditional clue must be used (via modus tollens) to force uniqueness
items.difficulty_rationale: A strong Difficulty 4. Four people must be matched one-to-one with four topics under five clues, one of which is a conditional. The non-conditional clues alone leave the puzzle underdetermined: they force Cara=Volcano and Ben=Robot but leave Ada's topic ambiguous between Comet and Engine. The solver must combine a derived fact (Ben chose Robot, so 'Ben chose Engine' is false) with the conditional clue and apply modus tollens to eliminate Ada=Comet, giving Ada=Engine. That integration step — carrying an intermediate result into a conditional rather than reading the answer off a straight elimination — is what makes it a genuine Difficulty 4. It is NOT Difficulty 5, however, because once Ben=Robot is forced the remaining work is a single modus tollens over a tiny model space (only two candidate assignments to rule between); there is no large search and no multiple interacting conditionals to manage. Arithmetic-free, four entities, mobile-readable.
items.prompt:
Four students — Ada, Ben, Cara, and Dan — each chose a different project topic: Volcano, Robot, Comet, or Engine. From the following, all true:

- Ada chose Comet or Engine.
- Cara chose Volcano.
- Ben did not choose Comet.
- Ben did not choose Engine.
- If Ada chose Comet, then Ben chose Engine.

Which topic did Ada choose?
items.solution: Cara chose Volcano. Ben did not choose Comet, Engine, or Volcano (Volcano is Cara's), so Ben chose Robot. Now use the conditional: 'If Ada chose Comet, then Ben chose Engine.' Ben chose Robot, so 'Ben chose Engine' is false; therefore the condition 'Ada chose Comet' must also be false (if it were true, Ben would have to be Engine). So Ada did not choose Comet. Since Ada chose Comet or Engine, Ada chose Engine. (Dan then takes the remaining topic, Comet.)

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: Ben = Robot makes the conditional's consequent false, so by modus tollens Ada is not Comet; with 'Comet or Engine', Ada = Engine.
distractor_rationales.B: Comet: chosen by narrowing Ada to {Comet, Engine} from clue 1 but failing to apply the conditional clue that rules Comet out — the most common miss.
distractor_rationales.C: Robot: confuses Ada with Ben, who is the one forced to Robot.
distractor_rationales.D: Volcano: confuses Ada with Cara, who is given Volcano.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure required. Render the setup sentence, then the five clues as a bulleted list (one per line, the conditional clue last), then the question. A 4x4 person-by-topic tick grid could optionally be offered as a scratch aid, but it is not necessary to state the puzzle and should be omitted from the stem to avoid mobile clutter.

option_descriptions.A: Topic: Engine.
option_descriptions.B: Topic: Comet.
option_descriptions.C: Topic: Robot.
option_descriptions.D: Topic: Volcano.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Five short bullet lines plus a one-line question fit mobile width with at most minor scrolling. Options are single words; render as four chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A matching puzzle assigning four students to four project topics under five clues, with four topic answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Constraint-satisfaction matching with an embedded conditional measures integrated deductive reasoning: the solver must maintain partial assignments, derive an intermediate fact, and feed it into a conditional to resolve the remaining ambiguity. This goes beyond single-rule application and beyond pure elimination.
items.knowledge_requirements: None. Topic names are arbitrary labels; no knowledge of volcanoes, robots, comets, or engines is used.

ambiguity_review.notes: Uniqueness verified. Cara = Volcano (clue 2). Ben is excluded from Comet (clue 3), Engine (clue 4), and Volcano (taken), so Ben = Robot — forced. Remaining topics Comet and Engine go to Ada and Dan. Clue 1 limits Ada to {Comet, Engine}. Clue 5 (Ada=Comet -> Ben=Engine): since Ben=Robot, the consequent is false, so by modus tollens Ada is not Comet; hence Ada = Engine and Dan = Comet. This is the only assignment satisfying all five clues: without clue 5 there would be two solutions (Ada=Comet/Dan=Engine and Ada=Engine/Dan=Comet), so clue 5 is load-bearing and the solver must use it. Exactly one answer (Engine). Each distractor is a real person-topic pairing in or near the solution (a genuine slip), not an alternative consistent assignment.

source_basis: Constraint-satisfaction / matching deduction (analytical-reasoning grid-puzzle tradition)
Conditional elimination by modus tollens within a multi-constraint model (deductive-reasoning item design; Johnson-Laird mental-model reasoning)

items.subskill: Identifying an instrument-to-action relation and matching it while rejecting reversed or mistyped relations
items.difficulty_rationale: A single, clearly stated relationship — an instrument paired with the action it is used to perform — applied to one new pair. The distractors are the three standard analogy errors: the relation reversed, an instrument paired with a substance instead of an action, and an object paired with a property. One mapping step, common words, no obscure vocabulary. Easy end of the range.
items.prompt:
KNIFE is to CUT as ___ is to ___.

(A knife is an instrument used to cut. Choose the pair with the same relationship.)
items.solution: A knife is an instrument used to perform the action 'cut'. The same relationship is 'a pen is an instrument used to perform the action write' — instrument to its action. That is option A.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: instrument (pen) paired with the action it performs (write), matching knife-to-cut.
distractor_rationales.B: Reverses the relation: this is action-to-instrument, the mirror image of the target.
distractor_rationales.C: Wrong relation type: a pen paired with the substance it uses (ink), not with the action it performs.
distractor_rationales.D: Wrong relation type: an instrument paired with one of its properties (sharp), not with an action.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the analogy stem on one line with the parenthetical relation cue beneath it in slightly muted text. Present options as four word-pairs using a colon between the two words.

option_descriptions.A: Pair: pen and write.
option_descriptions.B: Pair: cut and knife.
option_descriptions.C: Pair: pen and ink.
option_descriptions.D: Pair: scissors and sharp.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Short word-pairs; render as four chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A verbal analogy with the stem knife to cut, and four word-pair answer choices.

items.construct_rationale: Verbal analogies measure analogical reasoning — inferring a relation from one pair and mapping it onto another. Stating the relation explicitly removes vocabulary load so the item tests the mapping, not word knowledge.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond the everyday meanings of the listed common words.

ambiguity_review.notes: Target relation is fixed by the stem and the parenthetical: instrument -> action performed with it. Only A (pen -> write) instantiates it. B is the reverse (action -> instrument); C is instrument -> substance used; D is object -> property. None of B, C, D is an instrument-to-action pair, so exactly one option matches. The relation is stated, so no taste or interpretation is involved.

source_basis: Analogical reasoning as relation inference and mapping (Sternberg 1977, componential analysis of analogies)
Relation-direction and relation-type distractors as standard analogy item design

items.subskill: Grouping by underlying function and resisting a salient surface category
items.difficulty_rationale: Three of the four words name devices whose function is to RESIST or STOP motion; the fourth names a device whose function is the opposite — to PRODUCE motion. The intended basis (function) is not the most salient one: the words also split by domain (air, land, water), which tempts a domain-based answer. Medium difficulty comes from having to abstract the functional category and override the surface domain split, while the functional binary (produces motion vs opposes motion) isolates exactly one word.
items.prompt: Three of these words go together in a clear way, and one does not. Which word does NOT belong with the others?
items.solution: An anchor stops a boat from drifting, a brake stops a vehicle from rolling, and a parachute slows a falling person — all three are devices whose purpose is to oppose or stop motion. A sail does the opposite: it harnesses wind to CREATE motion. On the function 'opposes motion versus produces motion', the sail is the only one that produces motion, so it does not belong.

distractor_rationales.A: anchor: belongs — its function is to stop motion (drifting). Tempting only if one groups by 'used while moving', but an anchor still opposes motion like the brake and parachute.
distractor_rationales.B: brake: belongs — its function is to stop motion.
distractor_rationales.C: Correct: a sail produces motion rather than opposing it, the only word on that side of the functional split.
distractor_rationales.D: parachute: belongs — it slows a fall. Tempting if one groups by domain (it is the only 'air' item), but by function it joins the motion-stoppers.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the instruction line, then the four single words as four selectable options. Keep the words in lower case and equally weighted so no word is visually emphasized.

option_descriptions.A: Word: anchor.
option_descriptions.B: Word: brake.
option_descriptions.C: Word: sail.
option_descriptions.D: Word: parachute.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Four short words; render as chips, 2 columns on mobile.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A choose-the-odd-one-out item with four words: anchor, brake, sail, parachute.

items.construct_rationale: Verbal classification measures the inductive ability to find the organizing category behind a set. Using a functional category that competes with a salient surface category targets abstraction rather than mere association.
items.knowledge_requirements: Only the everyday meanings of the four common words; no specialist or cultural knowledge.

ambiguity_review.notes: Intended basis: function with respect to motion. Anchor, brake, parachute all OPPOSE/stop motion; sail PRODUCES motion. This binary places exactly one word (sail) on the minority side, so the odd one is unique under the intended basis. Checked rival bases: by domain (water/land/air) the four split 1-1-1-1 with sail and brake both arguably 'land/wind', giving no single odd word; by 'used during motion vs at rest', anchor is borderline but still functions to oppose motion, so this basis does not yield a cleaner single answer than the functional one. No rival basis isolates a different single word as cleanly, so sail is the defensible unique answer and the choice does not depend on taste.

source_basis: Verbal classification / odd-one-out as an inductive-reasoning measure (Carroll 1993, Induction factor)
Functional versus perceptual categorization and salient-feature override (categorization research on abstract grouping)

items.subskill: Separating what is strictly entailed from converse, biconditional, and necessary-vs-sufficient over-readings
items.difficulty_rationale: A three-sentence passage gives one borrowing rule, one labelling rule, and one fact. Exactly one conclusion is strictly entailed; the three distractors are the classic invalid extensions — treating the labelling rule as a biconditional, taking its converse, and treating a necessary condition as sufficient. The difficulty is structural: the solver must notice that the labelling rule yields nothing strict about Maria's specific book, and that only the borrowing rule plus the fact forces a conclusion. Short passage, common words; the work is in resisting plausible but unentailed readings.
items.prompt:
Read the passage, then answer using ONLY the information given.

At the Hill Street Library, any book that is borrowed is not a reference book. Every reference book in the library has a black label on its spine. Maria borrowed a book from the library yesterday.

Which statement MUST be true?
items.option_a: The book Maria borrowed is not a reference book.
items.option_b: The book Maria borrowed does not have a black label on its spine.
items.option_c: Every book with a black label is a reference book.
items.option_d: Every book without a black label can be borrowed.
items.solution: Any book that is borrowed is not a reference book, and Maria borrowed a book, so her book is not a reference book — option A. The labelling rule (every reference book has a black label) tells us only about reference books; it does not say non-reference books lack black labels, so we cannot conclude anything about the label on Maria's book.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: 'any book that is borrowed is not a reference book' applies directly to Maria, who borrowed a book, so her book is not a reference book.
distractor_rationales.B: Not entailed: the rule says reference books HAVE black labels, not that only reference books do. A non-reference book could still have a black label, so this treats the rule as a biconditional.
distractor_rationales.C: Not entailed: this is the converse of 'every reference book has a black label' and does not follow from it.
distractor_rationales.D: Not entailed: the passage says borrowed books are non-reference, not that every non-reference (or unlabelled) book may be borrowed. Inferring borrowability from the absence of a label reverses the borrowing rule — treating 'not a reference book' as sufficient for borrowing.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the instruction line, then the three-sentence passage as one short block, then the question. Keep the passage visually distinct (e.g., indented or boxed) from the question and options. Options are full sentences.

option_descriptions.A: Statement: Maria's book is not a reference book.
option_descriptions.B: Statement: Maria's book has no black label.
option_descriptions.C: Statement: every black-labelled book is a reference book.
option_descriptions.D: Statement: every book without a black label can be borrowed.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Three short sentences plus four sentence-length options; render options single-column full-width on mobile for readability.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A short library passage about borrowing rules and labels, followed by four statements to evaluate for what must be true.

items.construct_rationale: Drawing only strictly entailed conclusions from a short text measures deductive verbal reasoning and the discipline to avoid plausible-but-invalid inference — a core component of careful reading and reasoning, distinct from recall or interpretation.
items.knowledge_requirements: None beyond ordinary reading. No knowledge of libraries is required; both rules are stipulated in the passage.

ambiguity_review.notes: Formalization: borrow(x) -> not reference(x); reference(x) -> blacklabel(x); borrow(Maria's book). Modus ponens on the first rule gives not reference(Maria's book) = option A, strictly entailed and the only entailed option. B fails: reference -> blacklabel does not give not reference -> not blacklabel (no biconditional), so the label on a non-reference book is unknown. C fails: it is the converse of the labelling rule. D fails: borrow -> not reference does not yield its converse (not reference -> borrowable), so 'no label -> borrowable' does not follow. Exactly one option must be true; the others are each a named invalid inference, not a defensible alternative reading. Wording was revised from 'a book may be borrowed only if it is not a reference book' to 'any book that is borrowed is not a reference book' so the stated fact (Maria actually borrowed a book) connects directly to the rule, closing the permission-versus-actual-action gap.

source_basis: Textual entailment / necessary inference as deductive verbal reasoning (entailment-based reasoning tradition)
Converse, biconditional, and necessary-vs-sufficient errors as canonical invalid inferences (conditional-reasoning research)

items.subskill: Abstracting the logical form of an argument and matching the same form while rejecting valid forms and a different fallacy
items.difficulty_rationale: The stimulus commits a specific fallacy — affirming the consequent — and the task is to find the option with the SAME logical form. This requires stripping away the wording and representing each of four arguments abstractly, then matching forms. The distractors are engineered to defeat shallow strategies: one is a VALID argument in the same subject matter (matches the words, not the form), one is a DIFFERENT fallacy (denying the antecedent, which catches solvers who detect 'flawed' without identifying which flaw), and one is another valid form with a negative conclusion. Genuine integrated structural reasoning that cannot be done by keyword matching — legitimately ceiling-level verbal reasoning. The honest risk (see batch note) is reading load: four mini-arguments must stay scannable on mobile, and the A-versus-C discrimination is hard by design, not by trickery.
items.prompt:
Consider this argument:

'Every student who studied passed the test. Sara passed the test. So Sara must have studied.'

This argument is flawed. Which of the arguments below is flawed in the SAME way (same underlying structure)?
items.option_a: Every cake made with sugar tastes sweet. This pudding tastes sweet. So this pudding was made with sugar.
items.option_b: Every student who studied passed the test. Tom studied. So Tom passed the test.
items.option_c: Every student who studied passed the test. Bea did not study. So Bea did not pass the test.
items.option_d: No careless worker was promoted. Sam was promoted. So Sam was not careless.
items.solution: The stimulus has the form: if studied then passed; Sara passed; therefore Sara studied. It concludes the 'if' part from the 'then' part — affirming the consequent, which is invalid. Option A has exactly this form: if made with sugar then tastes sweet; this tastes sweet; therefore made with sugar. So A is flawed in the same way. Option B is valid (if studied then passed; Tom studied; so Tom passed). Option C is a DIFFERENT flaw — denying the antecedent (Bea did not study, so did not pass). Option D is valid (no careless worker was promoted; Sam was promoted; so Sam was not careless). Only A matches the stimulus's structure.

distractor_rationales.A: Correct: same form as the stimulus — affirms the consequent (tastes sweet, therefore made with sugar).
distractor_rationales.B: Valid argument (affirms the antecedent / modus ponens). Chosen by matching the studying-and-passing wording instead of the logical form.
distractor_rationales.C: A genuine fallacy, but a DIFFERENT one — denying the antecedent. Chosen by solvers who notice it is flawed without identifying that the stimulus specifically affirms the consequent.
distractor_rationales.D: Valid argument (modus tollens form). Chosen by matching the negative conclusion or mistaking a valid argument for a flawed one.

visual_asset_spec.main_figure_description: No figure. Render the stimulus argument as one short quoted block, then the instruction, then the four option arguments each as a single short sentence-group. Keep each option to one line where possible; use consistent sentence rhythm across options so structure, not wording, stands out. Do not bold any logical words.

option_descriptions.A: Argument about sugar and sweetness concluding the pudding has sugar.
option_descriptions.B: Argument concluding Tom passed because he studied.
option_descriptions.C: Argument concluding Bea did not pass because she did not study.
option_descriptions.D: Argument concluding Sam was not careless because he was promoted.

visual_asset_spec.mobile_behavior: Stimulus plus four one-sentence arguments. Render options single-column full-width on mobile; keep each argument short enough to avoid wrapping past two lines. This item carries the most reading, so generous line spacing is important.
visual_asset_spec.alt_text: A flawed sample argument followed by four candidate arguments, asking which has the same flawed structure.

items.construct_rationale: Parallel-reasoning (and parallel-flaw) items measure the ability to abstract argument form and reason about logical structure independent of content — one of the most demanding verbal-reasoning skills, drawing on deductive reasoning rather than vocabulary or recall.
items.knowledge_requirements: None. All content is everyday; no knowledge of formal logic terms is required, and the stimulus is identified as flawed so the task is structural matching.

ambiguity_review.notes: Formalize with P = antecedent, Q = consequent. Stimulus: P->Q; Q; therefore P (affirming the consequent, invalid). A: sugar->sweet; sweet; therefore sugar = P->Q; Q; therefore P (same flaw) — MATCH. B: studied->passed; studied; therefore passed = P->Q; P; therefore Q (modus ponens, valid) — different. C: studied->passed; not studied; therefore not passed = P->Q; not P; therefore not Q (denying the antecedent, a DIFFERENT invalid form) — different. D: 'no careless worker was promoted' = careless -> not promoted, i.e. promoted -> not careless; Sam promoted; therefore not careless = modus ponens on the contrapositive form (valid) — different. Exactly one option (A) shares the stimulus's form. B and D are valid (so not 'flawed in the same way'); C is flawed but by a distinct mechanism. The single correct answer is forced by structure, not interpretation.

source_basis: Parallel-reasoning and parallel-flaw argument analysis (analytical/logical-reasoning item tradition)
Affirming the consequent versus denying the antecedent as distinct conditional fallacies (conditional-reasoning research; standard fallacy taxonomy)
